title: Born at Phoebe Putney
summary: Interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Albany, Georgia, on March 9, 2013, Robert McClary recalls being born on October 19th at Phoebe Putney Hospital, his mother dying when he was young, and his father, who worked a job on the river, moving what was left of the family.

title: Learning to Walk at Seven
summary: McClary describes the family falling into poverty after his mother's death, being raised with a sister and brother by his father, learning to walk at about seven years old, the trouble over whether he would go to school, and how the men were paid every two weeks for their work.

title: He Knocked the White Man Down
summary: He recounts his father, who had come up from South Carolina, being insulted by a white man and knocking him down in a confrontation, and remembers attending a two-room school with a football field he had to cross.

part: The Movement Comes to Southwest Georgia
title: A New School and the Albany Movement
summary: McClary describes a high school newly built for Black families, the first families chosen to begin integrating, an incident involving a young white boy and a Black girl, and the early stirring of what became known as the Albany Movement before he himself got involved.

title: Charles Sherrod and Nine Counties
summary: He tells of writing for the movement paper and meeting Charles Sherrod, the plan to organize roughly nine counties across Southwest Georgia and take over a county to elect local people, and joining the effort, before the interview pauses for a technical delay.

title: Tenants and Welfare
summary: After the recording resumes, McClary describes the everyday work he did with Charles Sherrod: helping tenants and people who were denied welfare, filing appeals when benefits were taken away, and the white residents who had largely left the area.

title: The Casino Cafe Closed Its Doors
summary: He recounts an attempt to desegregate a cafe in his hometown, remembered as a casino, where Black customers were directed to a back window, an activist who had come down from New York joined the effort, and the owner closed the place rather than serve them at the front door.

title: Attacked in the Office at Night
topic: A Night Raid and Its Aftermath
summary: McClary describes being attacked while sleeping at the movement office, a man forcing his way in during the night, his escape and the police taking the man away that same night, and afterward learning skills like typing as part of the project.

title: Self-Defense and the Board
summary: Working for the Southwest Georgia project, McClary reflects on self-defense as something he would not retire from, and recounts a confrontation tied to a local board in a small town.

title: More Hopeful Than Afraid
topic: A First Black Official and His Father
summary: He recalls helping people meet challenges and the town electing its first Black official, says he was more hopeful and excited than afraid, points to his father as a man who would not back down to white intimidation, and the interview closes with thanks.
